skill.name: awesome-repo-builder
skill.description: Create a topic-specific GitHub awesome-list repository scaffold with a README, contribution rules, AI-agent instructions, URL checker, license, and reusable templates.
skill.summary: Generate a focused awesome-list repository from a topic, taxonomy, criteria, and seed resources.
skill.author: zjsxply

security_audit.summary: Manual review dismissed the critical static heuristic: there is no confirmed malicious intent, prompt injection, credential exfiltration, shell execution, or weak cryptography. The real risks are expected for this skill: it runs a bundled repository generator, writes files under a chosen output path, and creates a URL checker that can make outbound HTTP requests when executed.

medium_findings.title: Generated URL Checker Makes Outbound Requests
medium_findings.description: The bundled generator writes a verify_urls.py script that extracts HTTP(S) links from README.md and opens each URL with urllib. This is legitimate link-checking behavior, but it can send requests to arbitrary URLs from generated or edited README content if the checker is run.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The script template directly imports urllib.request and calls urlopen on URLs parsed from README.md. The behavior is transparent and task-related, but it is real outbound network access.

medium_findings.title: User-Selected Output Directory Receives Generated Files
medium_findings.description: The generator creates directories and writes README, contribution, license, gitignore, URL-checker, and template files under the user-provided output path. This is expected for a repository scaffold, but users should choose the output path carefully, especially when using --force.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The file writes are explicit and central to the skill purpose. Risk depends on the chosen path and whether --force is used, so the concern is moderate rather than high.

low_findings.title: Static Command-Execution Findings Are Mostly False Positives
low_findings.description: The static analyzer matched Markdown backticks, inline file names, and generated template text as shell execution. No evidence found of subprocess, os.system, eval, shell=True, or backtick execution in the Python generator; the only command is a documented manual Python invocation.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Manual review found formatting backticks and ordinary string rendering, not runtime shell execution. The documented command runs the bundled Python script with user-selected paths.

low_findings.title: Environment File Access Finding Is a False Positive
low_findings.description: The .env reference appears inside generated .gitignore content so scaffolded repositories ignore local environment files. No evidence found that the skill reads, parses, transmits, or writes secrets from environment files.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The referenced .env string is only emitted into .gitignore. There is no code path that opens or reads an environment file.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Findings Are False Positives
low_findings.description: The analyzer appears to have matched substrings in Markdown file names, text, or generated README content. No evidence found of MD5, SHA1, DES, insecure random generation, password hashing, or cryptographic operations.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Manual review found documentation and Markdown generation at the cited locations, not cryptographic API usage. The script imports only standard parsing, path, and typing modules outside the generated URL checker.

dangerous_patterns.title: Generated URL Checker Makes Outbound Requests
dangerous_patterns.description: The bundled generator writes a verify_urls.py script that extracts HTTP(S) links from README.md and opens each URL with urllib. This is legitimate link-checking behavior, but it can send requests to arbitrary URLs from generated or edited README content if the checker is run.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The script template directly imports urllib.request and calls urlopen on URLs parsed from README.md. The behavior is transparent and task-related, but it is real outbound network access.

content.value_statement: Creating a useful awesome list requires structure, criteria, and consistent contributor guidance. This skill generates a ready repository scaffold with curated sections, templates, and link verification support.
actual_capabilities: Builds a README-centered awesome-list repository from a topic and taxonomy.
Generates concise CONTRIBUTING.md and AGENTS.md guidance for maintainers and AI agents.
Creates reusable planning, implementation, checklist, and agent instruction templates.
Adds a standalone Python URL checker for README links.
Supports seed entries, related lists, inclusion criteria, exclusions, and nested sections.
Uses only Python standard library code in the bundled generator.
limitations: It does not publish the repository to GitHub or configure remote settings.
It does not guarantee that researched seed entries are exhaustive or permanently valid.
The generated URL checker makes network requests only when someone runs it.
It may overwrite generated files if the user selects an existing directory with --force.

use_cases.title: Launch a new technical awesome list
use_cases.description: Create a clean repository structure, README sections, and contribution rules for a new curated topic.

use_cases.title: Organize research resources
use_cases.description: Turn papers, tools, benchmarks, and related lists into a categorized resource catalog with notes.

use_cases.title: Standardize AI-assisted repository work
use_cases.description: Generate AGENTS.md and reusable templates so future AI edits follow clear repository conventions.

prompt_templates.title: Create a basic awesome list
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner request with a clear topic.
prompt_templates.prompt: Create an awesome-list repository for privacy-preserving machine learning. Propose a simple taxonomy, draft inclusion criteria, and generate the scaffold in ./awesome-privacy-ml.

prompt_templates.title: Use my taxonomy and criteria
prompt_templates.scenario: User already knows the repository structure.
prompt_templates.prompt: Build an awesome-list repository for developer productivity tools. Use these sections: editors, terminal tools, task runners, documentation, and measurement. Exclude general project management products.

prompt_templates.title: Seed with researched entries
prompt_templates.scenario: Intermediate request that needs initial research.
prompt_templates.prompt: Research credible resources for LLM evaluation harnesses, then create an awesome repository with 20 seed entries. Prefer official docs, papers, and active GitHub projects.

prompt_templates.title: Customize a production-ready scaffold
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced request with repository governance requirements.
prompt_templates.prompt: Generate an awesome-list scaffold for robotics simulation datasets. Include related awesome lists, nested taxonomy sections, strict inclusion and exclusion criteria, and concise AI-agent instructions.

output_examples.input: A request to build an awesome list for AI coding harnesses.
output_examples.output: A local repository folder with README, CONTRIBUTING, AGENTS, license, URL checker, and reusable task templates.

output_examples.input: A topic plus taxonomy, criteria, and several seed resources.
output_examples.output: A categorized awesome-list README with explanatory notes, contribution guidance, related lists, and verification instructions.

output_examples.input: A request to prepare a curated research catalog from scratch.
output_examples.output: A focused first version with researched seed entries, clear scope boundaries, and documented assumptions.

best_practices: Provide a topic, taxonomy, inclusion criteria, and exclusion criteria before generating the scaffold.
Review generated README entries for scope, quality, and source credibility before publishing.
Run the generated URL checker only in an environment where outbound requests are acceptable.
anti_patterns: Do not ask it to create a broad, exhaustive catalog without scope boundaries.
Do not run the generated URL checker on untrusted README content in a sensitive network.
Do not use --force against directories that contain hand-authored files you have not backed up.

faq.question: Does this skill create a complete GitHub repository?
faq.answer: It creates the local repository files for an awesome list. It does not create the remote GitHub repository.

faq.question: Can I provide my own resources?
faq.answer: Yes. You can provide seed entries, taxonomy sections, related lists, and inclusion criteria.

faq.question: Does it research resources automatically?
faq.answer: The workflow asks the AI assistant to research seed entries when the user does not provide them.

faq.question: What files does it generate?
faq.answer: It generates README, CONTRIBUTING, AGENTS, LICENSE, .gitignore, verify_urls.py, and several reusable templates.

faq.question: Does the generated URL checker use the network?
faq.answer: Yes. When run, it opens HTTP and HTTPS links found in README.md to verify reachability.

faq.question: Is it safe to use in an existing directory?
faq.answer: Use caution. The script writes generated files to the output path and --force can overwrite generated targets.
